topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias sat at his worn oak desk, the glow of his monitor reflecting in his wide, unblinking eyes. The manuscript lay open, a familiar landscape suddenly marred by alien syntax. Every carefully constructed sentence, every nuanced character arc he had poured his soul into, was mirrored, almost perfectly, in the text of 'The Shadow Weaver,' the work of his idol, Seraphina Vance. A coldness began to creep up his spine, a slow, heavy pressure that made his breath catch in his throat. He reread a passage, his hand trembling slightly as he traced the words with a fingertip. The air in the small apartment felt suddenly thin, suffocating. He pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the floorboards, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound sense of disorientation wash over him.

[story 2]

"This can't be right." Clara whispered, the sound barely audible above the hum of the refrigerator. She stared at the screen, her knuckles white where she gripped the mouse. The similarities were too precise, the thematic echoes too undeniable. It was the prose of Julian Thorne, her literary hero, repurposed, hollowed out and then meticulously refilled with her own emotional core. A tremor started in her hands, making the keyboard feel slick and foreign beneath her touch. She leaned back in her chair, a sharp intake of breath escaping her lips. For several long minutes, she simply sat there, motionless, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming inside her chest. When she finally moved, it was a slow, deliberate turn toward the bookshelf, her gaze fixed on the spine of Thorne’s latest novel, a deep furrow etched between her brows.

[story 3]

Marcus paced the length of his living room, the floorboards protesting with each heavy stride. He stopped abruptly, planting his feet as if anchoring himself against a sudden internal upheaval. The evidence was undeniable: the structural integrity of his favorite narrative had been dismantled and rebuilt using his own unique scaffolding. He ran a hand through his hair, feeling the dampness of sweat against his scalp. A tightness constricted his chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. He walked over to the framed photograph of the author, his jaw clenched so tightly he could feel the strain in his molars. The world seemed to tilt slightly on its axis; the colors of the room sharpened into an almost painful intensity, and everything felt fundamentally unstable.
